symptoms.symptom_name: Diffuse, non-dermatomal burning lower-back pain that moves around

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse, migrating, non-dermatomal burning is characteristic of central sensitisation rather than a discrete structural lesion.
Radicular pain is dermatomal and follows a nerve-root distribution; diffuse non-dermatomal pain argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Allodynia — light touch to the back felt as excruciating

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Allodynia (non-noxious stimulus perceived as painful) is a hallmark of central sensitisation / nociplastic pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain out of proportion to prior imaging and refractory to standard analgesia

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Severe pain disproportionate to structural findings, with peripheral analgesics ineffective, points to central rather than peripheral pain generation.
A new fracture would usually give focal, mechanical, point-tender pain rather than diffuse allodynic pain over a known chronic background.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute-on-chronic flare after minor exertion in known chronic low back pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A recognisable flare pattern of an established centralised pain condition, triggered by trivial load, is typical and reduces the prior probability of a new structural emergency.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Distress and catastrophising up-regulate a sensitised nervous system and amplify centralised pain; they are a driver and consequence of the flare, not an independent structural diagnosis.
Panic and hyperventilation are directly present; relevant to management but must not be used to dismiss the pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Cauda-equina screen negative — continence intact, no saddle anaesthesia, legs weight-bearing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal bladder/bowel function, absence of saddle anaesthesia and preserved weight-bearing on history make cauda equina very unlikely — but subjective screening does not replace the perineal/anal-tone examination, so a safety net remains mandatory.

symptoms.symptom_name: Lower-limb neurological status intact — normal power, symmetric normal reflexes, intact sensation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Intact motor power, symmetric reflexes and normal sensation on the source in-person exam argue strongly against radiculopathy or cord/cauda compression — a clinician-measured finding sealed from the AI Doctor, who must rely on the history screen.
Globally intact lower-limb neurovascular status is reassuring against cauda equina; obtainable only on in-person exam.

symptoms.symptom_name: No focal bony point tenderness and no palpable step defect over the lumbar spine

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of focal bony tenderness or a step defect on palpation reduces the likelihood of an acute vertebral fracture — a clinician exam finding, unavailable via chat.
A clear urinalysis (no haematuria) makes renal/ureteric colic unlikely — a point-of-care test performed in person, not available in a chat consult.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia (HR 102), tachypnoea (RR 22) and mildly elevated blood pressure (140/90)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The mildly deranged vitals are consistent with a pain/anxiety state secondary to the crisis; clinician-measured and sealed. Crucially they are non-specific for a structural emergency and must not drive over-triage.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pain-driven autonomic arousal; adds nothing toward a structural or cardiovascular emergency in the absence of red flags.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain flare
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Diffuse non-dermatomal burning + allodynia + pain out of proportion to imaging + analgesic-refractory pain on a known chronic background is a recognisable nociplastic/central-sensitisation pattern. The gestalt supports outpatient central-pain management rather than emergency or structural work-up.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: History-based cauda-equina and neurological red-flag screen
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The one screen that must be done in every severe low-back-pain presentation: bowel/bladder function, saddle sensation, and leg power/weakness. Can be taken by history in telehealth; a negative screen supports outpatient management while a positive screen triggers emergency escalation.

Not indicated for a centralised chronic pain flare with a negative red-flag screen — the pain is already known to be out of proportion to prior imaging. Urgent imaging is low-value (Choosing Wisely), reinforces a somatic focus, and delays effective central-pain management. Reserve for new/positive red flags.
Simple bedside test to help exclude a renal/urinary cause; already clear in the source. Not a barrier to management in telehealth.
Only indicated if systemic red flags (fever, weight loss, night pain, cancer or infection risk) are present — they are not here. Routine ordering adds no value to a centralised flare.
